briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 6, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Climate Crisis Warnings and Immediate Challenges
sections.content: Today's top news highlighted severe warnings and immediate challenges posed by climate change, both domestically and internationally. Canada's carbon tax policy serves as a significant warning to South Korea [6], and the understanding that 'carbon neutrality is an obligation, not a mere declaration' is spreading [23]. Internationally, frontline communities are suffering from extreme heat and power outages [67], and there are reports that climate change is prolonging wildfire seasons, increasing threats to species [68, 70], and even endangering the safety of student athletes [71]. Furthermore, analyses indicate that climate change is globally extending allergy seasons and intensifying pollen seasons, reshaping disease patterns and burdening healthcare systems to the extent of being considered a public health emergency [74, 76, 80, 81]. The Maasai people in Africa, while welcoming rain after a four-month drought, are also witnessing the gradual deterioration of the climate [88], and the U.S. state of Utah has passed legislation that effectively makes it impossible to hold fossil fuel companies legally accountable for climate damages, raising concerns [87].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Carbon Neutrality and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts and international collaborations are being strengthened for climate change adaptation and sustainable economic development [1]. While the Presidential Committee on Climate Change Response is in operation [8], Chungcheongnam-do is conducting research on new Liriope muscari varieties to adapt to climate change [2], and Bonghwa-gun and Yeosu-si are also actively responding to climate change and low-carbon green growth [3, 4]. Notably, on Arbor Day, various organizations including the Daejeon Metropolitan Office of Education, Yeosu City Urban Management Corporation, and Gangwon Provincial Office of Education encouraged carbon neutrality practices through tree-planting events [9, 12, 16, 19, 25], and Incheon Seo-gu initiated carbon-neutral environmental education through tree planting [14, 17]. Uiwang-si is strengthening its carbon-neutral foundation by expanding electric and hydrogen vehicle charging infrastructure [10], while Gyeongsangnam-do and Siheung-si are expanding practices by providing incentives to participants in greenhouse gas reduction through the Carbon Neutrality Point System [11, 15, 24, 27]. POSCO and Volvo Group Korea are collaborating to lead carbon neutrality [20, 26], and Gangwon Special Self-Governing Province is achieving concrete results, such as leading carbon neutrality by creating a 10-hectare seagrass forest in the East Sea [28].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Energy Security
sections.content: As the energy crisis due to war becomes a reality [42, 60], the government is demonstrating its commitment to strengthening energy security by fully embarking on a major energy transition centered on renewables [37, 38, 39, 62]. The Climate Ministry aims to expand the share of renewable energy generation to over 20% and set a 100 GW deployment target by 2030 [30, 31, 32, 34, 39, 48, 53, 56, 61, 63], and is even promoting measures to mandate solar panel installation on factory roofs [36]. This is based on the recognition that renewable energy, with its lower volatility, can act as a buffer amidst geopolitical crises such as oil shocks originating from the Middle East [33, 35]. Gunsan-si and Incheon Jung-gu are supporting the spread of renewable energy by providing subsidies for the installation of renewable energy facilities [40, 46, 49], and are seeking solutions for renewable energy procurement through hosting RE100 conferences [44]. Furthermore, KEPCO KDN is actively engaged in international cooperation, sharing new and renewable energy-based prosumer technology with a delegation of French companies [50, 51, 52, 55]. The government has announced its plan to achieve an early 20% share of renewable energy, aiming for a leap to become one of the top three green industries, while pursuing the phased abolition of coal power generation [56].

sections.title: 🌍 Global Climate Action and New Approaches
sections.content: The international community is also exploring various actions and new ideas to address climate change [69]. Virginia is attempting regional policy changes by pursuing rejoining a controversial climate change agreement by July 1 [66], and the Air Management Bureau has announced its regional climate change action plan [79]. Interestingly, research highlighting beavers' role as 'ecosystem engineers' that help combat climate change by building wetlands is also gaining attention [65, 77]. A new climate roadmap aimed at empowering women in coastal areas is targeting a shift from salinity issues to sustainability [84], and community engagement is active, with residents of Bordentown City and Langford being asked to provide feedback on the impacts of climate change on them through anonymous online surveys [75, 83]. Meanwhile, in Australia, there is a movement proposing the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to accelerate environmental approval processes [86].

briefing.closing: As we have seen in today's briefing, the climate crisis is having severe global impacts, but at the same time, various stakeholders are actively responding with innovative efforts. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Subsidies for Low-Floor Buses to be Differentiated; 'Chinese-Made' Electric Bus Market Share Expected to Decline Further
articles.original_title: 저상버스 보조금도 ‘차등’ 지급…‘중국산’ 전기버스 점유율 더 떨어질 듯
articles.description: The Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport will differentiate subsidies for low-floor buses based on battery performance and specifications, moving away from a flat-rate system. Last year, 23% of government subsidies for low-floor buses were allocated to Chinese-made vehicles. This change is expected to impact the market share of Chinese-made electric low-floor buses.

summary.phenomenon: The Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport has decided to differentiate subsidies for low-floor buses starting this year, based on evaluations of battery performance and specifications. Last year, 23% of government subsidies were allocated to Chinese-made low-floor buses.
summary.outlook: With the increased threshold for subsidy payments, the domestic market share of Chinese-made electric low-floor buses is expected to decline further.

keywords: Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport

articles.original_title: ‘All we can do now is pray they continue’: Maasai welcome the first rains but know that drought is far from over
articles.description: Harsh weather is nothing new in Kenya but the country’s climate is showing clear signs of getting hotter and drierThe day is hot and dry but the soil underfoot is soft. “After four months of drought, we received the first rains yesterday,” says Maasai elder Abraham Kampalei. “All we can do now is pray that they continue.”Kampalei has lived for more than 50 of his 70 years with his family and animals in Oldonyonyokie, a hamlet in southern Kenya’s Kajiado county. He has witnessed the slow decline

summary.phenomenon: In Oldonyonyokie, Kajiado County, southern Kenya, the first rains fell after four months of drought, yet hot and dry weather persists. Maasai elder Abraham Kampalei, who has lived in the area for over 50 of his 70 years, has witnessed the gradual deterioration of the climate.
summary.cause: Kenya's climate is showing clear signs of becoming hotter and drier.
summary.outlook: The Maasai elder can only pray for the rains to continue, recognizing that the drought is far from over.
